4. Performance Limits of Hypothesis Testing

4.1. LRs with discrete components

When the distribution of under either or has discrete components (e.g. nonzero atoms), the OC-LRT might look discrete or discontinuous (Example 4.1, Example 4.2).

Example 4.1 from lecture

4.1.1. Effect on Bayesian LRT

For the Bayesian formulation, equality at the threshold may have a nonzero probability. The Bayes risk is the same for either decision, meaning there are at least two operating points on the OC-LRT corresponding to the optimum Bayesian rule. When equality occurs with probability zero, there is a unique operating point.

4.1.2. Effect on Neyman-Pearson LRT

There may not be a threshold that gives yields . Then the Neyman-Pearson problem corresponds to the point on the OC-LRT whose is the largest smaller than . However, the curve can lie below the line (random guessing):

4.3. Randomized Tests

We chose a randomized test that picks one of two s. Is there some other type of randomized test that has better performance?

We have the following Markov chain

(4.3)

which is to say that depends only on and not (okay duh).

We have the following big results:

Theorem 4.1 (Deterministic Bayesian rule is all you need). A randomized test cannot achieve a lower Bayes risk than the optimum LRT in binary Bayesian hypothesis testing.

[TODO] prove. The intuition is that for Bayes cost, for a given point , you either prefer , , or are indifferent, and randomizing the last case doesn’t do anything.

Theorem 4.2 (Neyman-Pearson Lemma, Full Version). Given hypotheses and and any , for some and , there exists a decision rule of the form

(4.4)

such that and for any decision rule satisfying .

[TODO] prove.

The intuition for randomized NP is same as in the deterministic case. We want to greedily allocate those with larger . Handle equality with a mixed test to preserve budget. Therefore deterministic operating points are contained in the randomized set, and randomization linearly interpolates across jumps (convexifies the deterministic LRT-OC).

We refer to the dependence of the best achievable on as the Neyman-Pearson function : . The deterministic and randomized LRT operating points agree where there the distribution of the likelihood ratio does not have discrete components under or .

4.4. Efficient Frontier of Operation

Since is nondecreasing, the efficent frontier is OC-LRT of Neyman Pearson which looks like a convex hull.

Some facts from optimality:

  • The Neyman-Pearson function lies above the line since it is better than the randomized decision rule where denotes probability of outputting .
  • The Neyman-Pearson function is concave. Otherwise we could create a new rule using interpolation and get a better rule.

  • [TODO] The slope of the Neyman-Pearson function is equal to the threshold of the correspondign LRT:

    (4.5)

Achievable region is the reflection of the efficient frontier across the line since you can swap the labels. Hence a perfect test and a shit test are equally hard to obtain.
